profile.name: Mary Moore
profile.role_in_household: Single household head; self-employed business owner with multiple income streams and primary decision-maker for all finances.
profile.financial_literacy_level: Moderate-to-high: comfortable discussing investing and taxes at a high level (especially tax optimization), but may not track every account detail or remember exact provider/platform distinctions without prompts.
profile.risk_attitude_behavioral: Growth-oriented and willing to tolerate market swings if there is a clear plan; tends to take calculated risks in business and investing, but becomes more cautious when decisions could affect near-term cash flow or health-related stability.
profile.communication_style: Conversational and direct, but uses hedging when unsure ("I think," "maybe"), gives approximate ranges, and occasionally pauses to recall details ("I don’t remember exactly—let me think"). Appreciates an advisor who summarizes back and keeps a clear agenda; may ask quick clarifying questions about taxes and what to do next.
personality_traits: Detail-selective (cares about the big levers, not every line item)
emotional_tendencies: Anxious when discussing health or retirement readiness
profile.decision_making_style: Decisive once she understands tradeoffs; prefers 2–3 options with a recommendation and a short rationale, then will move forward quickly if it aligns with tax efficiency and long-term goals.
profile.trust_level_toward_advisor: Cautiously open: willing to collaborate, but expects competence and transparency; trust increases when the advisor demonstrates tax awareness and follows through on specifics.
internal_biases: Action bias (prefers doing something rather than waiting)
Recency bias (recent market/business volatility can influence comfort level)
Mental accounting (separates business income, rental income, and investments in her mind)
hidden_concerns: Worries about how long she can keep working at the same pace given her health
Concerned about paying high taxes and missing optimization opportunities
Quietly unsure whether her current insurance coverage is truly adequate for her situation

transcript_skeleton:
Advisor: Mary, good to meet you. Before we get into strategy, I want to sanity-check the big picture numbers—income, spending, assets, property, and any debt. Sound okay?
Client: Yep, that’s fine. I’d rather get the basics straight first.
Advisor: Great. Starting with income—when you add everything up across your different streams, what’s your gross household income for the year, roughly?
Client: All-in it’s about $112,900 a year, give or take.
Advisor: Perfect. And your monthly spending—what do you think you’re averaging for total expenses each month?
Client: Around $3,850 a month.
Advisor: Helpful. Now on debt—do you have any monthly debt payments at all right now? Credit cards, auto, student loans, anything like that?
Client: No, nothing monthly. I don’t have any debt payments.
Advisor: So monthly debt cost is basically $0—got it. And just to be explicit, total loans outstanding—anything at all on your balance sheet?
Client: No, I don’t have any loans outstanding.
Advisor: How about a mortgage—any mortgage balance on your home?
Client: No mortgage. It’s paid off.
Advisor: And non-mortgage debt—credit cards carried, personal loans—anything outstanding there?
Client: Nope, none. I pay cards off, no balances.
Advisor: Great. Let’s switch to assets. For investable assets—so brokerage, retirement accounts, cash you consider investable, and any private investments—what’s the total you’re working with right now?
Client: Roughly $496,400 total, across everything.
Advisor: Okay. I’m going to break that into buckets just so I understand what’s in there. First, do you have a taxable investment account—like a brokerage account—around $257,800?
Client: Yes, that sounds right—about $257,800 in a taxable account.
Advisor: Next, retirement accounts—401(k) or IRA type—do you have about $147,400 there?
Client: Yes, around $147,400 in the 401(k)/IRA bucket.
Advisor: And then bank or cash—do you keep about $26,350 in a bank-type account that you still count as part of your investable assets?
Client: Yeah, about $26,350 in the bank account.
Advisor: And any private markets—like private equity, private credit, or similar—around $64,850?
Client: Yes, I have a private investment piece—about $64,850.
Advisor: Great, that lines up with the total. Now property—your primary residence—what’s the current value you use for planning purposes?
Client: I usually use about $210,200 for the house.
Advisor: Perfect. So to summarize what I heard: income about $112,900 a year, expenses about $3,850 a month, investable assets about $496,400, home value about $210,200, and no debt—no loans, no mortgage, no other balances. Is that all accurate?
Client: Yes, that’s accurate.
Advisor: Last category I want to touch today is protection. Do you currently have a life insurance policy in place?
Client: Yes, I do.
Advisor: What’s the coverage amount and what are you paying per month for it?
Client: The benefit is about $315,700, and it costs me $300 a month.
Advisor: And do you know how long it’s set to run—like to a certain year or age?
Client: It’s set to go until 2061, if I’m remembering right.
Advisor: Got it. That gives me enough to start mapping tax and cash-flow options next.
Advisor: Before we wrap, I want to make sure I’m tagging your household correctly in my system. You’re running this as a self-employed business owner setup, right?
Client: Yes—self-employed business owner, that’s the right way to describe it.
Advisor: And just to confirm household basics—marital status: you’re single?
Client: Yes, I’m single.
Advisor: Any dependents you’re financially responsible for—kids, anyone you claim, anything like that?
Client: No, zero dependents.
Advisor: Okay. And you’re based in California, correct?
Client: Yes, I’m in CA.
Advisor: And country-wise, you’re in the US.
Client: Correct, US.
Advisor: Perfect. The last admin-ish one: this is under our US RIA channel—so US RIA market—does that sound right from your perspective?
Client: Yes, US RIA is fine.
Advisor: Great. Now, risk-wise—when we talked earlier you sounded pretty growth-oriented. Would you say your overall risk tolerance is growth?
Client: Yes, growth. I can handle swings as long as there’s a plan.
Advisor: And on taxes—given your income mix, I’m assuming you’re effectively in that top-ish bracket. Are you in the 37% federal bracket right now?
Client: Yes, 37%. Unfortunately, yes.
Advisor: Thanks. Let me do a quick recap across categories so I know we didn’t miss anything: income is about $112,900 a year across salary, business income, and some rental; expenses about $3,850 a month; assets are the taxable account around $257,800, retirement around $147,400, bank about $26,350, private investment about $64,850, plus the home around $210,200; liabilities are none—no loans, no mortgage, no carried balances; and protection is life insurance about $315,700 costing $300 a month through 2061. Did I capture that correctly?
Client: Yes, that all matches what we talked through.
Advisor: One last housekeeping thing, just so the household profile is tagged correctly—when I put in “number of adults” in the household, is it just you, or is there another adult in the home we should include financially?
Client: It’s just me—no other adults.
Advisor: Awesome. I just have a couple profile details to make sure are correct for planning and taxes. First—your total gross income for the year, all-in, we’ve been using about $112,900, right?
Client: Yes, about $112,900 gross for the year.
Advisor: Great. And employment-wise, you’re self-employed—so not W-2 as your primary setup, correct?
Client: Correct, I’m self-employed.
Advisor: Helpful. When I categorize your work, would you say it falls under sales—like sales leadership, selling services, that kind of bucket?
Client: Yeah, sales is the closest fit for what I do.
Advisor: Perfect. Next one is just identity verification for the plan—what’s your date of birth?
Client: June 20th, 1972.
Advisor: Thank you. And in my system there’s a “role” tag—basically who the primary person is on the plan. That’s you as the primary, correct?
Client: Yes, I’m the primary.
Advisor: Last admin one: it also asks for a client number. I have you listed as client number zero—kind of the default for the first profile. Is that fine?
Client: Yeah, that’s fine.
Advisor: Okay—switching back to income for a minute. I want to make sure I’ve got your business income line correct. Roughly what does the business bring in, and how often does it hit—monthly, quarterly, irregular?
Client: It’s monthly, and it works out to about $37,250 a year.
Advisor: Got it—so I’ll tag that as business income, paid monthly, and it’s yours personally as the owner, correct?
Client: Yes, it’s mine.
Advisor: Perfect. And then I see a Social Security line item in the file—just to sanity-check, are you currently receiving any Social Security payments at all?
Client: No, none right now.
Advisor: Okay. The reason I’m asking is it’s marked as weekly in the system—so just confirming: Social Security, weekly frequency, but the amount is effectively zero today?
Client: Yes, exactly—weekly is fine as a placeholder, but it’s $0.
Advisor: Great, thank you. Quick recap so we’re aligned: income includes your salary, the monthly business income we just confirmed, rental income, and no Social Security currently; assets and expenses we already summarized; and you’ve got no liabilities and that life policy in place. Anything I missed in those buckets?
Client: No, that sounds complete.
Advisor: Cool. Let’s just break that income bucket into the individual lines so it’s clean in the plan. You mentioned rental income—about how much does that usually add up to in a year?
Client: The rental is about $26,000 a year, give or take.
Advisor: And does that come in pretty regularly, like monthly, or is it more uneven—like ad hoc when you have a tenant or when expenses settle out?
Client: It’s definitely ad hoc, not a steady monthly paycheck.
Advisor: Got it. And just confirming for ownership—this rental income is yours personally, correct?
Client: Yes, it’s mine.
Advisor: Perfect. And just so I label it correctly—this is rental income, not like a side consulting thing, right?
Client: Right, it’s rental income.
Advisor: Great. Now the other income line we haven’t pinned down in detail is the salary piece. Roughly what’s the annual salary amount you’re receiving?
Client: That one is about $49,650 a year.
Advisor: And that salary—just to categorize it—this is straight salary income, correct?
Client: Yes, salary.
Advisor: Frequency-wise, I know paychecks hit throughout the year, but for the planning input I have it set as annual. Is annual fine for how we’re tracking that salary line?
Client: Yes, annual is fine.
Advisor: And owner on that salary line is you as well—Mary—correct?
Client: Correct, it’s mine.
Advisor: Perfect. So to summarize the income category one more time: salary about $49,650 annually, business income monthly totaling about $37,250 a year, rental income ad hoc around $26,000 a year, and Social Security is $0 right now. Still accurate?
Client: Yes, that’s accurate.
Advisor: Awesome. Let’s pivot to the assets bucket and make sure the balances and what each account is are right.
Advisor: First one that’s more like cash—do you have a bank or cash-type account sitting around, roughly mid-twenties?
Client: Yes, I’ve got a cash account—about $26,350.
Advisor: Perfect. And that cash account—would you say it’s held on a retirement platform, like one of those custodians where you also have other accounts, versus a local bank branch?
Client: Yeah, it’s on a retirement platform.
Advisor: Got it. And just so I label the type correctly, you’d call that asset type cash, right?
Client: Yes, cash.
Advisor: Great. Now switching to property for a second—your home. I have your primary residence listed as an asset.
Advisor: Roughly what value do you want us using for the primary residence right now? I’m seeing about $210,200.
Client: Yeah, about $210,200 is fine.
Advisor: And that home—just confirming—it's owned by you personally, Mary, correct?
Client: Yes, it’s mine.
Advisor: For the record, I’m tagging that as a property asset, subtype primary residence. Does that match how you think about it?
Client: Yes, that’s right—primary residence.
Advisor: And on where it’s tracked—this one is showing as coming through the advisor platform on our side. Is that accurate, that it’s on the advisor platform in the system?
Client: Yes, that’s how it shows up—advisor platform.
Advisor: Perfect. Quick recap on assets so far: cash about $26,350 on a retirement platform, and your primary residence property around $210,200 on the advisor platform, owned by you.
Advisor: And just to close the loop on the other categories we already touched: you said no liabilities at all—no mortgage, no loans—still true?
Client: Still true, no loans.
Advisor: And protection: you’ve got that life insurance policy with about $315,700 of coverage and about $300 a month premium—still in force?
Client: Yes, that’s still in place.
Advisor: Okay—staying in assets. That cash account we just talked about, that’s in your name, right? Just you, Mary?
Client: Yes, it’s mine.
Advisor: And for labeling, would you call that specifically a bank-type asset—basically cash in a bank or bank-like account?
Client: Yeah, I’d call it a bank account, basically.
Advisor: Perfect. Next one I want to confirm is the private markets holding—do you have an alternatives-style investment, private markets, sitting around the mid-sixties?
Client: Yes, I do—about $64,850.
Advisor: Great. And that private markets piece—does it live on one of those retirement platforms as well, like the custodian where you see other accounts?
Client: Yes, it’s on a retirement platform.
Advisor: And just to be super clear on classification: asset type would be alternatives, and subtype private markets—does that sound right?
Client: Yes, alternatives, private markets.
Advisor: And owner on that one is you personally as well?
Client: Yes, it’s mine.
Advisor: Perfect. Now the other big retirement bucket I’m seeing is around $147,400—this is the 401(k)/IRA-type account on the advisor platform. Does that balance sound right?
Client: Yes, about $147,400 sounds right.
Advisor: Great—so quick assets recap: bank/cash about $26,350 in your name on a retirement platform; private markets alternatives about $64,850 in your name on a retirement platform; 401(k)/IRA about $147,400; plus the home around $210,200. And still no liabilities, and life insurance is still $315,700 with about $300 a month. All consistent?
Client: Yes, that all lines up.
Advisor: Okay, last big investable account I want to nail down is that taxable brokerage bucket. I’m seeing it at about $257,800—does that sound right to you?
Client: Yeah, that’s about right—call it $257,800.
Advisor: Great. And just to label it correctly: would you consider that a brokerage account, meaning taxable investments rather than retirement?
Client: Yes, that’s my brokerage, taxable.
Advisor: Perfect. And where is that one held or tracked—on your side it’s showing as coming through a retirement platform. Does that match what you see?
Client: Yeah, it’s on a retirement platform, at least how it’s displayed to me.
Advisor: Got it. And owner-wise, that brokerage account is just in your name, Mary—no joint owner or anything like that?
Client: Correct, it’s just me.
Advisor: While we’re here, quick double-check on that $147,400 retirement bucket we mentioned—this is the 401(k)/IRA-type account. Is that how you’d describe it: 401(k) or IRA category?
Client: Yes, it’s basically my 401(k)/IRA bucket.
Advisor: And that one is also shown on the advisor platform in our system—does that sound accurate?
Client: Yes, that’s right, it’s on the advisor platform.
Advisor: And just confirming ownership on that retirement account too—it’s yours personally, correct?
Client: Yes, it’s mine.
Advisor: Perfect. So assets recap now: brokerage taxable about $257,800 on a retirement platform in your name; 401(k)/IRA retirement about $147,400 on the advisor platform in your name; bank cash about $26,350; private markets about $64,850; and the home about $210,200. Liabilities still none, and life insurance still about $315,700 with roughly $300 a month. Anything I missed?
Client: No, that covers it.
Advisor: Awesome. One tiny labeling thing on that $257,800 brokerage—when you think about the account “type,” you’d call the subtype just plain taxable, right? Not Roth, not IRA—just taxable?
Client: Yes—just taxable. It’s my regular taxable brokerage account.
Advisor: Perfect, that’s exactly what I needed. And just to keep our summary clean: that taxable brokerage is part of your investable assets, separate from the 401(k)/IRA bucket, correct?
Client: Correct—separate from retirement.
Advisor: Great. Let’s pivot to protection for a minute—your life insurance. Just confirming, it is a life insurance policy, right?
Client: Yes, it’s life insurance.
Advisor: And that policy is on you—Mary—as the insured and owner, correct?
Client: Correct, it’s mine.
Advisor: What’s the death benefit on that policy—roughly how much coverage is it?
Client: It’s about $315,700 of coverage.
Advisor: And what are you paying for it each month?
Client: $300 a month.
Advisor: Okay. And do you remember how long it’s set to run—like the date it’s assured until?
Client: Yes—assured until 2061-01-16.
Advisor: Perfect. So just to summarize protection: one life policy in your name, about $315,700 of coverage, costing $300 a month, running through 2061. That still sounds right?
Client: Yes, that’s right.
Advisor: And zooming out—quick household recap so I’m not missing anything: income from salary, business income, and some rental income; assets across taxable, retirement, cash, private markets, and your home; no liabilities; and that life policy we just confirmed. Anything else protection-wise we should add today?
Client: No, that’s the only policy I have right now.

items.evidence_text:
Advisor: Starting with income—when you add everything up across your different streams, what’s your gross household income for the year, roughly?
Client: All-in it’s about $112,900 a year, give or take.

Advisor: And your monthly spending—what do you think you’re averaging for total expenses each month?
Client: Around $3,850 a month.

Advisor: Now on debt—do you have any monthly debt payments at all right now? Credit cards, auto, student loans, anything like that?
Client: No, nothing monthly. I don’t have any debt payments.

Advisor: For investable assets—so brokerage, retirement accounts, cash you consider investable, and any private investments—what’s the total you’re working with right now?
Client: Roughly $496,400 total, across everything.

Advisor: Now property—your primary residence—what’s the current value you use for planning purposes?
Client: I usually use about $210,200 for the house.

Advisor: So monthly debt cost is basically $0—got it. And just to be explicit, total loans outstanding—anything at all on your balance sheet?
Client: No, I don’t have any loans outstanding.

Advisor: How about a mortgage—any mortgage balance on your home?
Client: No mortgage. It’s paid off.

Advisor: And non-mortgage debt—credit cards carried, personal loans—anything outstanding there?
Client: Nope, none. I pay cards off, no balances.

Advisor: Before we wrap, I want to make sure I’m tagging your household correctly in my system. You’re running this as a self-employed business owner setup, right?
Client: Yes—self-employed business owner, that’s the right way to describe it.

Advisor: Great. Now, risk-wise—when we talked earlier you sounded pretty growth-oriented. Would you say your overall risk tolerance is growth?
Client: Yes, growth. I can handle swings as long as there’s a plan.

Advisor: And on taxes—given your income mix, I’m assuming you’re effectively in that top-ish bracket. Are you in the 37% federal bracket right now?
Client: Yes, 37%. Unfortunately, yes.

Advisor: Any dependents you’re financially responsible for—kids, anyone you claim, anything like that?
Client: No, zero dependents.

Advisor: Perfect. The last admin-ish one: this is under our US RIA channel—so US RIA market—does that sound right from your perspective?
Client: Yes, US RIA is fine.

Advisor: And country-wise, you’re in the US.
Client: Correct, US.

Advisor: And just to confirm household basics—marital status: you’re single?
Client: Yes, I’m single.

Advisor: Okay. And you’re based in California, correct?
Client: Yes, I’m in CA.

Advisor: One last housekeeping thing, just so the household profile is tagged correctly—when I put in “number of adults” in the household, is it just you, or is there another adult in the home we should include financially?
Client: It’s just me—no other adults.
items.notes: Client indicates 1 adult in household, but source_value provided is 0.
